content_markdown:
Three statements about Boltzmann's constant $k_{\text{B}}$ are:

I. $k_{\text{B}}$ has a unit of $\text{J K}^{-1}$

II. $k_{\text{B}} = \frac{\text{gas constant}}{\text{Avogadro's constant}}$

III. $k_{\text{B}} = \frac{\text{the average kinetic energy of particles}}{\text{temperature of the gas}}$

Which statements are correct?

diagram_metadata.description: No diagram present, only text-based statements and options.

vibe_explanation: Statement I is correct because $k_{\text{B}}$ relates energy to temperature ($E = \frac{3}{2}k_{\text{B}}T$). Statement II is correct by definition ($k_{\text{B}} = R/N_{\text{A}}$). Statement III is incorrect because the average kinetic energy is $\frac{3}{2}k_{\text{B}}T$, meaning $k_{\text{B}} = \frac{2}{3} \frac{\bar{E}_k}{T}$, so the ratio of energy to temperature is not simply $k_{\text{B}}$ but includes a numerical factor.
